channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: The Job That Didn't Exist Last Year #ai #jobs #workwithai
executive_summary: Gemini 3 is reshaping AI workflows and job roles in ways that were unimaginable just a year ago. The focus is on routing AI models to the right workflows, making tasks more efficient and integrated. This shift means that teams that adapt will outperform those that stick to traditional methods.
bullets: Gemini 3 is changing the landscape of AI strategy by emphasizing that 'the best model wins' is an oversimplification. The real challenge lies in effectively routing models to the right workflows. Think of it like a traffic system where the best roads don't matter if the cars aren't directed to them properly.

Tasks that require constant human oversight, referred to as 'eyes-on-glass' tasks, are becoming AI-native territory. This means that jobs which once needed human monitoring can now be automated, freeing up human resources for more complex tasks. It's like having a security camera that not only records but also alerts you when it detects movement, allowing you to focus on more critical issues.

In AI-driven teams, the terms 'specification' and 'review' have evolved significantly. Specification now involves clearly defining what the AI should achieve, while review means assessing AI outputs for accuracy and relevance. Imagine writing a recipe where you not only list ingredients but also specify how each ingredient contributes to the final dish's flavor.

Product, engineering, and marketing teams are gaining new leverage through the integration of Gemini 3. This means that these departments can now collaborate more effectively, utilizing AI to enhance their workflows. Think of it like a sports team where every player understands their role and works together seamlessly to win the game.

Teams that learn to articulate intent when working with AI will create a significant competitive advantage. This involves clearly communicating what they want the AI to achieve and reviewing its outputs accordingly. It's like teaching a dog commands; the clearer you are, the better the dog performs.

The video emphasizes that the gap between teams that merely test AI chat outputs and those that rethink entire workflows is widening. This means that organizations must adapt to survive in the evolving AI landscape. It's like the difference between a company that just sells products and one that innovates and creates new markets.

Nate B. Jones highlights that Gemini 3's impact is not just about technology but also about changing how teams operate. This shift requires a cultural change within organizations to embrace AI fully. Think of it like a company transitioning from a traditional office to a fully remote setup; it requires new tools and mindsets.

The video suggests that organizations need to rethink their entire workflow to fully leverage AI capabilities. This means not just implementing AI but integrating it into every aspect of the business. It's like renovating a house; you can't just paint the walls and expect everything to work better.

By the end of the year, companies that adopt Gemini 3 will likely see significant improvements in efficiency and productivity. This timeline emphasizes the urgency for teams to start adapting now. It's like preparing for a marathon; the sooner you start training, the better your chances of finishing strong.

The speaker notes that many teams currently have an evaluation test split of 80% functionality and only 20% hygiene. This is backwards; at least 50% of evaluation tests should focus on code hygiene, including naming, structure, and function length. Think of it like a car; if you only check if it runs but ignore the oil change, it won't last long.

Gemini 3 allows for more effective collaboration across departments, which can lead to innovative solutions and faster problem-solving. This means that teams can leverage AI to enhance their creativity and efficiency. It's like a band where each musician plays their part, creating a harmonious sound together.

The video stresses that AI's role in workflows is not just about automation but also about enhancing human capabilities. This means that AI should be seen as a tool to augment human work rather than replace it. Think of it like a calculator; it doesn't replace the need for math skills but makes calculations faster and more accurate.
key_takeaways: Start integrating AI into your workflows immediately to stay competitive. The sooner you adapt, the more likely you are to see improvements in efficiency and productivity. Waiting could mean falling behind your competitors who are already leveraging AI.

Ensure that at least 50% of your evaluation tests focus on code hygiene, not just functionality. This will lead to more maintainable and robust code in the long run. If you ignore this, you risk creating technical debt that could hinder future development.

Encourage your team to articulate their intent clearly when working with AI. This will help in getting better outputs from the AI and create a more efficient workflow. Without clear communication, you may end up with results that don't meet your needs.

technical_terms.Gemini 3: A new AI model that reshapes workflows and job roles, focusing on routing AI to the right tasks. Think of it like a new engine that makes a car run more smoothly and efficiently.
technical_terms.Eyes-on-glass tasks: Tasks that require constant human monitoring, which are now being automated by AI. It's like having a lifeguard who can now use a drone to monitor the pool instead of watching every swimmer directly.
technical_terms.Specification: The process of clearly defining what the AI should achieve in a project. Think of it like creating a blueprint for a building; without it, the construction can go wildly off course.
technical_terms.Review: The act of assessing AI outputs for accuracy and relevance. It's like proofreading an essay; you need to check for errors and ensure it conveys the right message.
technical_terms.Workflows: The sequence of processes through which a piece of work passes from initiation to completion. Like a factory assembly line where each step is crucial for the final product.
technical_terms.Evaluation tests: Tests designed to assess the performance and quality of code or AI outputs. Think of it like a quality control check in a factory to ensure each product meets standards.
technical_terms.Code hygiene: The practice of maintaining clean, well-structured, and efficient code. It's like keeping a kitchen clean; a tidy space makes cooking easier and more enjoyable.
technical_terms.AI-native territory: Tasks or areas where AI is now the primary tool used instead of humans. Think of it like a farm where tractors have replaced manual labor for plowing fields.
technical_terms.Cultural change: The shift in mindset and practices within an organization to embrace new technologies like AI. It's like a company moving from a traditional office to a remote work environment; it requires new tools and ways of thinking.
technical_terms.Collaboration: Working together across different departments to achieve a common goal, especially with the help of AI. Think of it like a sports team where each player has a unique role but works towards winning the game together.
technical_terms.Intent articulation: The process of clearly expressing what you want the AI to achieve. It's like giving precise directions to a taxi driver; the clearer you are, the better the outcome.
technical_terms.Technical debt: The implied cost of additional rework caused by choosing an easy solution now instead of using a better approach that would take longer. Think of it like ignoring a small leak in your roof; it might be easier now, but it will lead to bigger problems later.
